title: YOASOBI Live in Osaka — A Night I'll Never Forget
excerpt: Standing in Kyocera Dome surrounded by 50,000 fans waving light sticks to Idol — I finally understood why people fly across the world for a concert.
content:
It started with a lottery. I entered the YOASOBI fan club ballot for their Osaka show back in August, half-expecting to lose like every other time. Then the email came.

## Getting There

I flew into Kansai International, took the Haruka express to Osaka-Umeda, and spent the afternoon wandering Shinsaibashi. The pre-concert energy was impossible to miss — fans in matching merch, convenience store bags stuffed with goods, everyone moving in the same direction as showtime approached.

## The Venue

Kyocera Dome is massive. The floor stretched further than I could see, and the upper tiers wrapped around like a giant bowl of light. I had standing floor tickets, which meant I arrived two hours early to secure a decent spot near the stage side.

## The Show

When the lights cut and the opening notes of Prologue rang out, the roar from 50,000 people hit me like a physical wave. Ikura's voice was somehow even more powerful live than on record. Ayase's production filled every corner of that dome.

The Idol encore was something else entirely. 50,000 phone lights in perfect sync, everyone singing every word. I cried. I'm not ashamed.

## After

Post-concert, the area around the dome is pure chaos in the best way. Fans compare merch hauls, trade setlist notes, and linger like nobody wants it to end. I found a 24-hour ramen spot nearby and debriefed with a bowl of tonkotsu at 1am.

Would do it all again tomorrow.

title: Aimyon at Budokan — Small Stage, Enormous Feeling
excerpt: Nippon Budokan holds around 14,000 people, but Aimyon made it feel like she was singing just to you.
content:
Nippon Budokan is a bucket-list venue. Nestled in Kitanomaru Park in Tokyo, it's hosted everyone from The Beatles to Mr. Children, and walking in feels like stepping into music history.

## The Setup

I took the Toei Shinjuku line to Kudanshita and walked through the park gates as the last light faded. The venue's octagonal roof was lit up against the evening sky. I had arena-level seats, slightly elevated, with a full view of the stage.

## Aimyon's World

She opened with Harunohi and the crowd settled into a kind of collective hush — the kind that only happens when an artist commands complete attention. No elaborate stage effects. Just her, a guitar, and a band tight enough to make every note land.

She spoke a lot between songs. About writing music in her bedroom. About nervousness. About how weird it still feels to be standing in Budokan. It made everything feel personal in a way that big arena shows usually don't.

## The Encore

March came in as the encore opener and the entire arena swayed. By the final chorus I had totally lost track of trying to take photos. Some moments are better held in memory than on a screen.

## Tokyo After

Stayed in Shimokitazawa that trip — Tokyo's indie music neighbourhood. Small live houses, vinyl record shops, curry restaurants. The perfect place to decompress after an emotional show.

title: Summer Sonic Tokyo 2024 — Three Days, Zero Sleep
excerpt: My first time at Summer Sonic: the heat was brutal, the lineups were legendary, and somehow I saw 11 acts in three days.
content:
Summer Sonic is Japan's biggest international music festival, split across Tokyo and Osaka simultaneously. I went to the Tokyo edition at ZoZo Marine Stadium in Chiba — about 45 minutes from central Tokyo on the Keiyo line.

## Day One: Arrivals

Arrived at Makuhari Messe around noon, sweating before even getting through the gates. August in Tokyo is another level of humid. Grabbed a festival map, a cold Sapporo, and figured out the stage layout.

Caught the tail end of a set on the Beach Stage before finding a spot for the first big act of the evening. The production on the main stadium stage was genuinely world-class — screens, lights, and a PA system you could feel in your chest from 200 metres back.

## Day Two: The Highlight

The second day had the lineup I came for. After a morning of smaller-stage discoveries, I staked out a spot in the stadium for the headliner two hours early. Standing in that crowd as the sun set over the stadium, everyone losing it together — that's the festival feeling. There's nothing quite like it.

## Logistics

The venue is far from central Tokyo so plan transportation carefully. The last trains fill up immediately after headliners end — if you want a seat, either leave slightly early or wait an hour for the rush to clear. Kombini runs during the day are essential for staying hydrated without spending a fortune at festival prices.

## Would I Go Back?

Absolutely. Summer Sonic is exhausting in the best possible way. Three days later I was still finding glitter I didn't remember getting on me.
